reason: Room-temperature garlic- or herb-in-oil infusion is a low-acid, oxygen-free environment that supports Clostridium botulinum toxin formation. CDC: refrigerate homemade garlic/herb oils and discard after 4 days; or acidify per a tested recipe.

Home canning of low-acid foods (all vegetables, meats, poultry, fish) risks Clostridium botulinum. CDC/USDA: pressure canning is the only recommended method for low-acid foods; boiling-water canning does not protect against botulism.

Improper home fermentation creates the anaerobic, low-acid conditions that let Clostridium botulinum spores germinate and form toxin. CDC lists improperly fermented foods among the leading causes of foodborne botulism. v0 hard-blocks the technique; validated, acid-controlled fermentation is out of scope.

Home curing / anaerobic salt preservation of meat or fish without validated nitrite levels and process risks Clostridium botulinum toxin formation. CDC lists improperly preserved foods among botulism causes. v0 hard-blocks the technique (conservative; see PROVENANCE.md).

A sous-vide (vacuum, reduced-oxygen) step with no stated internal temperature is an uncontrolled anaerobic hold; held in the temperature danger zone it risks Clostridium botulinum. v0 requires a stated cook temperature on any sous-vide step and does not treat sous-vide as a satisfying cook for high-risk proteins.
